mnemonics.title: Use Case Components
mnemonics.content: Remember Use Case description has: NAPS-EA - Name, Actors, Preconditions, Steps (Basic+Alt), Exceptions, Assumptions. Alternatively: PABS - Preconditions, Actors, Basic flow, System response (postconditions).

mnemonics.title: Include vs Extend
mnemonics.content: INCLUDE = INcorporated always (mandatory). EXTEND = EXTra behavior (optional/conditional). Think: INCLUDE = INtegration, always needed. EXTEND = Extension, optional add-on.

acronyms.usage: Standard language for visualizing, specifying, constructing, and documenting software systems
Description of system's behavior from user's perspective
Actor who initiates use case to achieve a goal
Actor who assists but doesn't initiate the use case
Primary path through a use case without errors
Secondary paths for variations or exceptions

visualTips.visualization: Draw actors as stick figures on the outside, system boundary as a rectangle, use cases as ovals inside. Connect actors to use cases with solid lines (associations). Use dashed arrows for <<include>> and <<extend>>.
Arrow always points TO the thing being used/included. For include: base → included. For extend: extending → base. Remember: 'I need to INCLUDE something INTO my base' = base points to included.
Use a solid triangular arrowhead pointing from child to parent (general case). Like inheritance in class diagrams - arrow points to the superclass/parent.

analogies.analogy: Just as a movie script describes scenes (scenarios) of how characters interact, a use case describes scenarios of how actors interact with the system to achieve goals.
The system boundary is like a company's gate - everything inside (employees, offices, systems) is part of the company, while visitors (actors) are outside but interact with the company.
Think of <<include>> like LEGO blocks - reusable pieces that are always needed to build the main structure. They cannot exist on their own.
<<extend>> is like adding optional features to an app - the base app works without them, but they extend functionality under certain conditions (like dark mode on specific screens).
